blocks.desc: End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
L1. Pre-instruction math diagnostic. NJSLS prerequisites for the upcoming challenge math. Not graded — flags students needing closer monitoring.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L45. Guides give direct, honest feedback on every draft. Does the nutritional argument hold? Can every member defend their section? Are charts legible and labeled? Is the cost model traceable to real sources? Teams revise immediately after.

L32. Where the menu fails to hit nutritional targets — which day, which nutrient. Students identify the gap and draft a specific request back to the chef. 5/6: guide helps translate analysis → polite request. 7/8: students draft the follow-up communication, guide reviews before sending.
blocks.ttl: Gap analysis: which day, which nutrient

blocks.desc: L3. Digestive chemistry: amylase on starch, stomach acid on protein, bile on fat, absorption across the small intestine wall. Demonstrate the iodine test live so the chemistry is concrete. 5/6 stays at the demo level; 7/8 engages with enzyme/bond-breaking mechanism and surface area.

Added. Guide reads every team's draft brief, gives direct feedback. Are nutritional targets realistic and traceable to the FDC work? Are the chef questions specific enough to get useful answers? Teams revise after.
blocks.ttl: Guide review of draft briefs (live feedback)

blocks.desc: Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L37 (combined with menu finalization). Send the gap-analysis follow-up to the chef. Get responses. Lock the final 5-day menu. After this block, no more menu changes.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L15 + L16 combined. Scale the full recipe to 30 servings AND produce the shopping list from the scaled card in one continuous block. 5/6: same-system quantities only — guides pre-convert metric. 7/8: full conversions (incl. cross-system) by students. Output: a priced-ready shopping list for the grocery store visit.

L33 (cooked). Same tests on cooked samples — what changes when an ingredient is cooked? Compare to the raw results from earlier in the challenge. Strengthens the team's claim about what the menu actually delivers nutritionally.

L24 instructional half. Build the bar chart that becomes the team's visual: what goes on each axis, how to label, what makes a chart legible vs cluttered. Then apply to one dataset (cost or nutrition) in the same block.
blocks.ttl: Bar chart construction: axes and labeling

blocks.desc: L36. Seminar on a short reading about food and the body. Reading assigned the day before with 2–3 questions due on arrival. 15 min discussion max. Reinforces what's already been taught — does not introduce new content.
blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 1 on food and the body

blocks.desc: L46. Every team member presents their section and answers simulated panel questions. Guides play the panel and ask hard questions. Any student who cannot answer goes back and learns the material before the real panel.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L9 split (2 of 2). What each nutritional field means and how to read it. Students look up three foods they actually eat and write a paragraph each on what the data says. Bring results to the next session.

External event. Each team meets their restaurant partner in person. Present the brief, hear chef's dish proposals, ask the three prepared questions, take notes. May span two days depending on partner availability.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
L27 part. What a client brief is and why it exists. Guide models a complete brief using a fictional restaurant. Sets up the team-draft block that follows immediately.
L29 split (2 of 2). Role-play the meeting. Every student introduces themselves and asks one of their brief questions out loud before they ever do it for real. Guides play the chef.

Added (Week 6). How to make a professional call: how to introduce yourself, how to ask about pricing, how to record what you learn, how to handle a 'we'll call you back'. Practice on each other before any real supplier call.

L20 split (1 of 2). What labor cost is in a professional kitchen, why it often exceeds ingredient cost. Estimate prep time with the chef's input. Conceptual + estimation only — calculation lives in the next block.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

External event. Wholesale supplier comes in (or team calls). Students ask specific questions about their own ingredient lists and record answers. Phone-etiquette lesson runs before this so they're ready.

L24 application. Bar chart of cost-per-student-per-meal across the 5 days, with the $10 ceiling marked. Student-built using the chart skills from the construction lesson.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
Finalize the shopping list before the grocery visit. Confirm units match what stores actually sell in. Identify every conversion still needed at the store. No store visit until this list is clean.
L18 (G6). Same content as G5's retail vs wholesale block, but expressed as percentage difference / unit rate. Sets up the markup conversation for 7/8.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Continued team-draft work on the full presentation. Charts, evidence, defended claims. Every section traceable back to the source data the team gathered.

L21 split (1 of 2). Overhead = packaging + delivery + restaurant margin. Worked example. 5/6 takes overhead as a flat dollar add-on; 7/8 derives it as a percentage of ingredient cost (how restaurants actually compute it).

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
L36. Seminar on a short reading about why calories alone is a poor metric for nutrition. Same format as Reading 1. 5/6 gets a simpler/adapted version of both readings.
blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 2 on calories as limited metric

blocks.desc: End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L41. Edit kitchen-visit footage into a 60–90 second video for the partner's social media. Content serves the restaurant, not the student. Clean cuts and audible audio first; transitions and color come if there's time.

L27 + L28 combined. Each team drafts the brief for their actual restaurant partner: $10/meal ceiling, nutritional targets, dietary constraints, serving size 30, three specific questions for the chef. Drafts get reviewed before the meeting — this block produces the draft, not the final.
blocks.ttl: Client brief: model + team draft (I do / We do)

blocks.desc: L29 split (1 of 2). What a professional client meeting looks like. Introductions, what you need to learn before you leave, what to do when you don't understand the chef, how to take useful notes during the meeting.

L4. Macronutrients — what carbs, proteins, fats each do in the body. Not interchangeable. 5/6: carbs as fuel, protein as building material, fat as backup fuel. 7/8: same plus glycogen storage, amino-acid chains, lipid structure.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
L31 split (3 of 3). Lock in macronutrient totals for the whole 5-day menu. Compare to the targets the team set in week 1. Any discrepancies feed directly into gap analysis.

Decimal addition/subtraction on real ingredient prices. Fluency block — students need this solid before retail-pricing calculations land. Pull dollar figures from the actual grocery shopping list.
blocks.ttl: Decimal fluency: addition/subtraction on ingredient prices

blocks.desc:
Diagnostic, not a gate, of math and biology concepts covered in weeks 1-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide.

L33 (cooked). Same tests on cooked samples — what changes when an ingredient is cooked? Compare to the raw results from earlier in the challenge. Strengthens the team's claim about what the menu actually delivers nutritionally.

Panel Day 1. Five teams present + defend (~15 min each, including Q&A). Restaurant partners rotate as panel members for teams other than their own. Blind taste test runs in parallel — menus identified by number only.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
L13 split (1 of 2). Unit conversion within volume — teaspoon → tablespoon → cup → pint → quart. Use real recipe-card quantities. Same-system only. 7/8 reviews quickly and moves toward cross-system.
Added (5/6). Mean/median/mode applied to the actual cognitive data the class has been gathering. Pulls the statistics out of the abstract and into something they care about (their own scores).

Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L5. Glucose, energy, and cognitive performance. Simple-carb spike-and-crash vs sustained energy from complex carbs. 5/6: ground in their lived experience (candy → great → terrible) then the biology. 7/8: add insulin's role and glycemic index.

Final-pass revisions on the deck and the speaking parts based on guide-review feedback. Last chance to tighten before the simulated panel.
L38 split (3 of 3) (anchored). Safety + movement in a working kitchen. How to move without creating hazards, cleaning as you go, when to ask the chef vs improvise. Locked in before the kitchen visit.

L38 split (2 of 3). Knife skills: grip, basic cuts, safety. Practice in school with whatever blades are available before the kitchen visit. Students should not pick up a knife for the first time in a professional kitchen.
Final-pass revisions on the deck and the speaking parts based on guide-review feedback. Last chance to tighten before the simulated panel.

L9 split (1 of 2). USDA FoodData Central: how to navigate the database, find a whole food vs a packaged item, locate specific nutritional fields. Pure mechanics — students should be able to search and pull up a record fluently before interpretation comes in.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

External event. Full-day grocery store visit. Teams take their finalized shopping lists and price every ingredient at retail — price, package size, unit, total cost for the quantity their scaled recipe needs. The unit-conversion work from earlier weeks has to be solid or this visit fails.
Process the wholesale supplier visit. Enter wholesale pricing into the team's spreadsheet alongside retail. Sets up the retail-vs-wholesale lesson and the cost model.
blocks.ttl: Supplier visit debrief + wholesale data entry

L6. Gut-brain axis and specific nutrient–system connections: serotonin in the gut, iron and oxygen transport, omega-3s and adolescent brain dev, protein → neurotransmitters. Why 'calories' obscures all of this. 5/6: the connections they can feel (nervous → stomach). 7/8: name the neurotransmitters and their precursors.

L18 (G5). Retail vs wholesale: why the gap exists, what volume buying does to unit cost. G5 calculates the dollar difference for their own ingredient list. Comes after the wholesale supplier visit so they have real numbers to work with.

L12 application (G5). Apply fraction multiplication to the team's full recipe card, ingredient by ingredient. Same-system quantities only — guides pre-convert any metric quantities.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L25. Bar chart: macronutrient totals per day vs the team's agreed targets across the 5-day menu. Shows where the menu hits and where it falls short — drives the gap analysis lesson.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 4-5. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.

L9 split (1 of 2). USDA FoodData Central: how to navigate the database, find a whole food vs a packaged item, locate specific nutritional fields. Pure mechanics — students should be able to search and pull up a record fluently before interpretation comes in.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Practice Q&A on the cost model. Teams pitch their cost model to another team and answer hostile questions. Catches weak spots before guide review and panel.
Cost model finalization. Lock the draft. After kitchen visit, actual prep time replaces estimated and labor figures get revised. Final number defended at panel.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.
Continued team-draft work on the full presentation. Charts, evidence, defended claims. Every section traceable back to the source data the team gathered.
L21 split (2 of 2). Apply overhead to the team's own menu. Each day in the five-day menu gets an overhead line. Output: a complete cost line ready to assemble in cost-model build.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

L10 (combined). Set up the daily cognitive routine and run it once to confirm students know the procedure cold. Why we test, what each test measures (reaction time / Stroop / digit span), how the shared Sheet works, how lunch logging fits in — followed immediately by the first live 6-minute run. After this session, the routine runs every day at 12:45. (Folded together from former L10 split: setup + first-run.)

L45. Guides give direct, honest feedback on every draft. Does the nutritional argument hold? Can every member defend their section? Are charts legible and labeled? Is the cost model traceable to real sources? Teams revise immediately after.
L31 split (2 of 3). Continue nutritional analysis through the rest of the 5-day menu. Macronutrient totals per day across the week. Goal: data ready for gap analysis next session.

L9 split (2 of 2). What each nutritional field means and how to read it. Students look up three foods they actually eat and write a paragraph each on what the data says. Bring results to the next session.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L5. Glucose, energy, and cognitive performance. Simple-carb spike-and-crash vs sustained energy from complex carbs. 5/6: ground in their lived experience (candy → great → terrible) then the biology. 7/8: add insulin's role and glycemic index.
L20 split (2 of 2) (G5). Apply hourly rate × prep time / 30 to produce labor cost per student per meal. Simple arithmetic on numbers students already gathered.
L34. Run the four tests on samples from another team's menu. Cross-checking. Results go into both teams' presentation records — keeps every team accountable to a peer audit before the panel.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Added (5/6). Mean/median/mode applied to the actual cognitive data the class has been gathering. Pulls the statistics out of the abstract and into something they care about (their own scores).

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math and biology concepts covered in weeks 1-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L5 follow-up. Insulin and metabolic regulation: cellular uptake of glucose, why the post-spike overshoot happens, why diet is a legitimate cognitive-performance variable, not just a health one. Pair with cognitive data once it's accumulating.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 2-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate
L11+L12 combined (We do / You do). Apply ratios + unit rate to the full recipe card. Every team scales their actual recipe ingredient by ingredient. G7/G8 cohort.

Diagnostic, not a gate, of math concepts covered in weeks 2-3. Both cohorts in parallel with different items. Pass bar 5/6 correct. Failure triggers a targeted reteach determined in scope and length by the guide. 

Surfaces fluency gaps mid-Exploration with runway to remediate before the end-of-Exploration fluency gate

L33 (raw). Run all four chemical tests on raw ingredient samples from the team's menu. Record results, draft interpretive statements about what each test confirms and what it can't. Goes into the presentation record.
Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

blocks.ttl: Proportionality table: wholesale + retail as two lines

blocks.desc: Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.

L43 + L44 combined. Presentation structure (sections, owners, evidence flow, what panel will ask) followed by team draft assembly in the same block. Section assignments are guide-decided — no student self-selects. Younger students must own a substantive section.
Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L36. Seminar on a short reading about food and the body. Reading assigned the day before with 2–3 questions due on arrival. 15 min discussion max. Reinforces what's already been taught — does not introduce new content.
blocks.ttl: Seminar: Reading 1 on food and the body

blocks.desc: L43 + L44 combined. Presentation structure (sections, owners, evidence flow, what panel will ask) followed by team draft assembly in the same block. Section assignments are guide-decided — no student self-selects. Younger students must own a substantive section.

Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Decimal addition/subtraction on real ingredient prices. Fluency block — students need this solid before retail-pricing calculations land. Pull dollar figures from the actual grocery shopping list.
blocks.ttl: Decimal fluency: addition/subtraction on ingredient prices

blocks.desc: Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L22 split (2 of 2). Check every figure in the cost model against its source. If a number can't be traced, it gets fixed or removed. The traceability check is the actual assessment of whether the team did the underlying work themselves.
blocks.ttl: Cost model: verify each figure traces to source

blocks.desc: Daily anchor. Whole class movement and stand-up to start the day. Not instructional time.

L41. Edit kitchen-visit footage into a 60–90 second video for the partner's social media. Content serves the restaurant, not the student. Clean cuts and audible audio first; transitions and color come if there's time.

L7 + L8 combined into one 90-minute block (formerly 4 separate demo + practice sessions). Guide demo first on contrasting samples (whole foods vs processed) so the chemistry is concrete: iodine for starch (blue-black indicates starch), Benedict's for simple sugars (blue → orange → brick red with rising concentration when heated), brown paper spot test for fats (translucency under light), Biuret for protein (blue → purple). Students then run all four tests on a new sample set, recording results and writing interpretive statements. 5/6: simple statements ('starch present; can't tell how much'). 7/8: note degree of color change and what that suggests about relative concentration. Name the limit explicitly at the end: tests confirm presence and rough quantity, not grams. By end of session every student should be able to run all four tests independently.
blocks.ttl: Chem tests: demo + practice (iodine / Benedict's / brown paper / Biuret)

blocks.desc: End-of-day anchor. Reflect on what got produced, what's stuck, what's next. Not new instruction.

L31 split (1 of 3). First pass at nutritional analysis. Students enter day-1 menu items into FDC and pull macronutrient data per serving. 5/6: 3 macros + 2-3 key micros (iron, calcium). 7/8: full macros + 6-8 micros.
L31 split (3 of 3). Lock in macronutrient totals for the whole 5-day menu. Compare to the targets the team set in week 1. Any discrepancies feed directly into gap analysis.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.

Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L7 + L8 combined into one 90-minute block (formerly 4 separate demo + practice sessions). Guide demo first on contrasting samples (whole foods vs processed) so the chemistry is concrete: iodine for starch (blue-black indicates starch), Benedict's for simple sugars (blue → orange → brick red with rising concentration when heated), brown paper spot test for fats (translucency under light), Biuret for protein (blue → purple). Students then run all four tests on a new sample set, recording results and writing interpretive statements. 5/6: simple statements ('starch present; can't tell how much'). 7/8: note degree of color change and what that suggests about relative concentration. Name the limit explicitly at the end: tests confirm presence and rough quantity, not grams. By end of session every student should be able to run all four tests independently.

blocks.desc: L38 split (1 of 3). Mise en place: what it is, why pro kitchens run on it. How prep gets staged before any heat goes on. Set the cultural expectation before the visit, not at the visit.

blocks.ttl: Slope review: what if cost isn't fixed?

L30. Restaurant meeting debrief. What did you learn, what surprised you, what do you still need to find out, what math do you need to do now you have the actual recipe card. This lesson surfaces gaps before scaling work begins.

External event. Full-day visit to the partner's kitchen. Teams cook the finalized menu items under chef supervision — they prep, measure, execute. Not observation. Also shoot social-media footage where the partner permits.
blocks.ttl: FULL DAY: Kitchen visit (cook in partner kitchen)

blocks.desc: Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Process the wholesale supplier visit. Enter wholesale pricing into the team's spreadsheet alongside retail. Sets up the retail-vs-wholesale lesson and the cost model.
blocks.ttl: Supplier visit debrief + wholesale data entry

blocks.desc: Daily anchor. Eat the menu under design (or current rotation). Students log lunch in the cognitive performance Sheet.

L40 + L42 combined. Study the restaurant partner's existing online voice (Instagram, website). Write a brief style description. Then write captions for the team's static images and video in that voice. Caption must sound like the restaurant wrote it, not a student.
Stand-up is where students set their goals for the day in a group with their assigned guides.
Revise the brief based on guide review. Brief must be clean, specific, and answer-ready before the restaurant meeting. No team meets a partner with an unreviewed brief.
L12 'I do' for G5. Guide models scaling one full ingredient line from recipe-card unit to 30 servings on the board. Students follow only — independent work comes in the next block.

L2. The digestive system as a mechanical process: how food moves from ingestion through stomach to small intestine. 5/6: concrete language, factory-line analogy. 7/8: introduce peristalsis, bolus, chyme.
Daily routine. 6 minutes: reaction time (Human Benchmark), Stroop, digit span. Log scores + lunch in shared Sheet. Runs at the same time every day from week 2 onward.
Week 1. Teams and roles. Mixed-age team assignments are deliberate — older students are expected to teach younger ones throughout the challenge. The no-passenger rule is named and explained here.

L6. Gut-brain axis and specific nutrient–system connections: serotonin in the gut, iron and oxygen transport, omega-3s and adolescent brain dev, protein → neurotransmitters. Why 'calories' obscures all of this. 5/6: the connections they can feel (nervous → stomach). 7/8: name the neurotransmitters and their precursors.

Panel Day 2 + individual mastery assessments + final reflection. Remaining five teams present. Then every student does a 10-min individual conversation with a guide on their menu's nutritional rationale, scaling math, and cost model — the no-passenger rule is enforced here. End with reflection + announcement of which menus enter the Forge lunch rotation.
blocks.ttl: FULL DAY: Panel Day 2 + mastery assessments + final reflection

blocks.desc: L46. Every team member presents their section and answers simulated panel questions. Guides play the panel and ask hard questions. Any student who cannot answer goes back and learns the material before the real panel.

L11. Introduce ratios before formal notation. 'If one person needs 4oz, how much do 30 need?' Reason through it intuitively first. 5/6: stay with whole-number examples. 7/8: move to formal notation (a:b, ratio-as-fraction) faster.
